[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0e/Post-Game_Developers_Conference_2010_-_Day_1_%40_Capcom_%284422912558%29.jpg/250px-Post-Game_Developers_Conference_2010_-_Day_1_%40_Capcom_%284422912558%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Post-Game_Developers_Conference_2010_-_Day_1_@_Capcom_(4422912558).jpg)

A video game developers' conference offers industry experts the chance to try new games and network.

The **video game industry** is a significant segment of the [leisure](/wiki/Leisure_industry "Leisure industry") sector, straddling the [tertiary sector](/wiki/Tertiary_sector "Tertiary sector"), which provides services to people, and the [quaternary sector](/wiki/Quaternary_sector_of_the_economy "Quaternary sector of the economy"), which focuses on knowledge-intensive activities such as research and technological development. This industry includes the [development](/wiki/Video_game_development "Video game development"), [marketing](/wiki/Marketing "Marketing"), [distribution](/wiki/Distribution_(marketing) "Distribution (marketing)"), [monetization](/wiki/Video_game_monetization "Video game monetization"), and [consumer feedback](/wiki/Customer_satisfaction_research "Customer satisfaction research") processes related to [video games](/wiki/Video_game "Video game"). The [industry](/wiki/Industry_(economics) "Industry (economics)") encompasses dozens of job disciplines and thousands of jobs worldwide.[[1]](#cite_note-1) The professions involved range from game designers and software engineers to sound designers, testers, marketers, and customer support staff. Video games have gradually gained increasing relevance as a widespread cultural phenomenon, exerting significant influence on many areas of contemporary society: from the economy and the labor market to education, from consumption patterns and daily habits to architecture and urban planning, passing through sectors such as healthcare, the automotive industry, cinema and television, fashion, and sports.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

The video game industry has grown from niche to mainstream.[[3]](#cite_note-3) As of July 2018[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Video_game_industry&action=edit), video games generated US$134.9 billion annually in global sales.[[4]](#cite_note-4) In the US, the industry earned about $9.5 billion in 2007, $11.7 billion in 2008, and US$25.1 billion in[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Video_game_industry&action=edit) 2010,[[5]](#cite_note-5) as per the [ESA](/wiki/Entertainment_Software_Association "Entertainment Software Association") annual report. Research from Ampere Analysis indicated three points: the sector has consistently grown since at least 2015 and expanded 26% [from 2019 to 2021](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic"), to a record $191 billion; the global games and services market is forecast to shrink 1.2% annually to $188 billion in 2022.[[6]](#cite_note-6) Video games now compete with movies, music, and television in terms of both popularity and revenue.

The video game industry has played an important role in improvement of [computer hardware](/wiki/Computer_hardware "Computer hardware"). Many parts of modern [personal computers](/wiki/Personal_computer "Personal computer") were originally improved to meet the needs of video games. The industry has influenced the technological advancement of [personal computers](/wiki/Personal_computer "Personal computer") through [sound cards](/wiki/Sound_card "Sound card"), [graphics cards](/wiki/Graphics_card "Graphics card") and [3D graphic accelerators](/wiki/3D_graphic_accelerator "3D graphic accelerator"), [CPUs](/wiki/CPU "CPU"), and co-processors like [PhysX](/wiki/PhysX "PhysX").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Sound cards, for example, were originally developed for games and then improved for adoption by the [music industry](/wiki/Music_industry "Music industry").[[7]](#cite_note-7)

## Industry overview

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_industry&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Industry overview")]

### Size

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_industry&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Size")]

In 2017 in the United States, which represented about a third of the global video game market, the [Entertainment Software Association](/wiki/Entertainment_Software_Association "Entertainment Software Association") estimated that there were over 2,300 development companies and over 525 publishing companies, including in hardware and software manufacturing, service providers, and distributors. These companies in total have nearly 66,000 direct employees. If including indirect employment, such as a developer using the services of a graphics design package from a different firm, the total number of employees involved in the video game industry rises above 220,000.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

### Value chain

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_industry&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Value chain")]

Traditionally, the video game industry has had six connected layers in its [value chain](/wiki/Value_chain "Value chain") based on the retail distribution of games:

1. [Game development](/wiki/Video_game_development "Video game development"), representing programmers, [designers](/wiki/Video_game_designer "Video game designer"), and [artists](/wiki/Game_art_design "Game art design"), and their leadership, with support of [middleware](/wiki/Middleware "Middleware") and other development tools.
2. [Publishing](/wiki/Video_game_publisher "Video game publisher"), which typically includes both the source of funding the development of a video game, as well as providing the marketing and advertising for a game.
3. Distribution, whether through retail or digital channels. Distribution typically includes manufacturing and duplication of game media and packaging for retail games.
4. Retailer, storefront where the game is sold.
5. Consumers, the purchasers and players of video games
6. Hardware platform manufacturers, which can own and place limitations for content on the platform they have made, charging license fees to developers or publishers.

As games have transitioned from the retail to more digital market, parts of this value chain have become redundant. For example, the distributor may be redundant as a function of either the publisher or the retailer, or even in some cases as the case of [indie games](/wiki/Indie_game "Indie game"), the function of the developer themselves.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

### Roles

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_industry&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Roles")]

Ben Sawyer of Digitalmill observes that the development side of the industry is made up of six connected and distinctive layers:

1. Capital and publishing layer: involved in paying for development of new games and seeking returns through licensing of the properties.
2. Product and talent layer: includes [developers](/wiki/Video_game_developer "Video game developer"), [designers](/wiki/Video_game_designer "Video game designer") and [artists](/wiki/Game_art_design "Game art design"), who may be working under individual contracts or as part of in-house development teams.
3. Production and tools layer: generates content [production tools](/wiki/Game_tool "Game tool"), game development [middleware](/wiki/Game_Middleware "Game Middleware"), [customizable game engines](/wiki/Game_engine "Game engine"), and production management tools.
4. Distribution layer: or the "publishing" industry, involved in generating and marketing catalogs of games for retail and online distribution.
5. Hardware (or Virtual Machine or Software Platform) layer: or the providers of the underlying platform, which may be console-based, accessed through online media, or accessed through mobile devices such as smartphones. This layer includes network infrastructure and non-hardware platforms such as virtual machines (such as Java or Flash), or software platforms such as browsers or Facebook.
6. End-users layer: or the players of the games.[[10]](#cite_note-newmedia-10)

The game industry employs those experienced in other traditional businesses, but some have experience tailored to the game industry. Some of the disciplines specific to the game industry include: [game programmer](/wiki/Game_programmer "Game programmer"), [game designer](/wiki/Game_designer "Game designer"), [level designer](/wiki/Level_designer "Level designer"), [game producer](/wiki/Game_producer "Game producer"), [game artist](/wiki/Game_artist "Game artist"), and [game tester](/wiki/Game_tester "Game tester"). Most of these professionals are employed by [video game developers](/wiki/Video_game_developer "Video game developer") or [video game publishers](/wiki/Video_game_publisher "Video game publisher"). However, many [hobbyists](/wiki/Hobby "Hobby") also produce computer games and sell them commercially.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Game developers and publishers sometimes employ those with extensive or long-term experience within the [modding](/wiki/Mod_(video_gaming) "Mod (video gaming)") communities.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

### 1940s–1960s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_industry&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: 1940s–1960s")]

Further information: [Early history of video games](/wiki/Early_history_of_video_games "Early history of video games") and [Early mainframe games](/wiki/Early_mainframe_games "Early mainframe games")

Prior to the 1970s, there was no significant commercial aspect of the video game industry, but many advances in computing would set the stage for the birth of the industry. Many early publicly available interactive computer-based game machines used or other mechanisms to mimic a display; while technically not "video games", they had elements of interactivity between the player and the machine. Some examples of these included the 1940 "Nimatron", an electromagnetic relay-based [Nim](/wiki/Nim "Nim")-playing device designed by [Edward Condon](/wiki/Edward_Condon "Edward Condon") and built by [Westinghouse Electric](/wiki/Westinghouse_Electric_(1886) "Westinghouse Electric (1886)") for the [New York World's Fair](/wiki/1939_New_York_World%27s_Fair "1939 New York World's Fair"),[[12]](#cite_note-Priest-12) *[Bertie the Brain](/wiki/Bertie_the_Brain "Bertie the Brain")*, an arcade game of [tic-tac-toe](/wiki/Tic-tac-toe "Tic-tac-toe"), built by [Josef Kates](/wiki/Josef_Kates "Josef Kates") for the 1950 [Canadian National Exhibition](/wiki/Canadian_National_Exhibition "Canadian National Exhibition"),[[13]](#cite_note-CNE-13) and [Nimrod](/wiki/Nimrod_(computer) "Nimrod (computer)") created by engineering firm [Ferranti](/wiki/Ferranti "Ferranti") for the 1951 [Festival of Britain](/wiki/Festival_of_Britain "Festival of Britain").[[14]](#cite_note-14)

The development of [cathode-ray tube](/wiki/Cathode-ray_tube "Cathode-ray tube"), the core technology inside televisions, created several of the first true video games. In 1947, Thomas T. Goldsmith Jr. and Estle Ray Mann filed a patent for a "cathode ray tube amusement device". Their game, which uses a cathode-ray tube hooked to an oscilloscope display, challenges players to fire a gun at target.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

Between the 1950s and 1960s, with mainframe computers becoming available to campus colleges, students and others started to develop games that could be played at terminals that accessed the mainframe. One of the first known examples is *[Spacewar!](/wiki/Spacewar! "Spacewar!")*, developed by Harvard and MIT employees Martin Graetz, [Steve Russell](/wiki/Steve_Russell_(computer_scientist) "Steve Russell (computer scientist)"), and Wayne Wiitanen.[[16]](#cite_note-Space-16) The introduction of easy-to-program languages like [BASIC](/wiki/BASIC "BASIC") for mainframes allowed for more simplistic games to be developed.

The [arcade video game](/wiki/Arcade_video_game "Arcade video game") industry grew out of the pre-existing [arcade game](/wiki/Arcade_game "Arcade game") industry, which was previously dominated by [electro-mechanical games](/wiki/Electro-mechanical_game "Electro-mechanical game") (EM games). Following the arrival of [Sega](/wiki/Sega "Sega")'s EM game *[Periscope](/wiki/Periscope_(arcade_game) "Periscope (arcade game)")* (1966), the arcade industry was experiencing a "technological renaissance" driven by "audio-visual" EM novelty games, establishing the arcades as a healthy environment for the introduction of commercial video games in the early 1970s.[[17]](#cite_note-Smith-17) In the late 1960s, a college student named [Nolan Bushnell](/wiki/Nolan_Bushnell "Nolan Bushnell") had a part-time job at an arcade where he became familiar with EM games such as [Chicago Coin](/wiki/Chicago_Coin "Chicago Coin")'s [racing game](/wiki/Racing_video_game "Racing video game") *Speedway* (1969), watching customers play and helping to maintain the machinery, while learning how it worked and developing his understanding of how the game business operates.[[18]](#cite_note-NGen23-18)

### 1970s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_industry&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: 1970s")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/99/Magnavox-Odyssey-Console-Set.jpg/250px-Magnavox-Odyssey-Console-Set.jpg)](/wiki/File:Magnavox-Odyssey-Console-Set.jpg)

The [Magnavox Odyssey](/wiki/Magnavox_Odyssey "Magnavox Odyssey") was released in 1972 as the first home video game console.

See also: [First generation of video game consoles](/wiki/First_generation_of_video_game_consoles "First generation of video game consoles"), [Video game crash of 1977](/wiki/Video_game_crash_of_1977 "Video game crash of 1977"), [Golden age of video arcade games](/wiki/Golden_age_of_video_arcade_games "Golden age of video arcade games"), and [Second generation of video game consoles](/wiki/Second_generation_of_video_game_consoles "Second generation of video game consoles")

In 1971, the first commercial [arcade video game](/wiki/Arcade_video_game "Arcade video game"), *[Computer Space](/wiki/Computer_Space "Computer Space")*, was released.[[19]](#cite_note-cleverism.com-19) The following year, [Atari, Inc.](/wiki/Atari,_Inc. "Atari, Inc.") released the first commercially successful video game, *[Pong](/wiki/Pong "Pong")*, and 19,000 [arcade cabinets](/wiki/Arcade_cabinet "Arcade cabinet") of the original arcade version were sold.[[20]](#cite_note-20) In that year, video games were introduced to the home market with the release of the early [video game console](/wiki/Video_game_console "Video game console"), the [Magnavox Odyssey](/wiki/Magnavox_Odyssey "Magnavox Odyssey"). However, both the arcade and home markets would be dominated by *Pong* clones, which flooded the market and led to the [video game crash of 1977](/wiki/Video_game_crash_of_1977 "Video game crash of 1977"). The crash eventually came to an end with the success of [Taito](/wiki/Taito "Taito")'s *[Space Invaders](/wiki/Space_Invaders "Space Invaders")*, released in 1978, inspiring the [golden age of video arcade games](/wiki/Golden_age_of_video_arcade_games "Golden age of video arcade games").[[21]](#cite_note-Whittaker-122-21) The game's success prompted the prevalence of arcade machines in mainstream locations such as [shopping malls](/wiki/Shopping_mall "Shopping mall"), traditional storefronts, [restaurants](/wiki/Restaurant "Restaurant"), and [convenience stores](/wiki/Convenience_store "Convenience store") during the golden age.[[22]](#cite_note-22) More than 360,000 *Space Invaders* [arcade cabinets](/wiki/Video_game_arcade_cabinet "Video game arcade cabinet") were sold worldwide,[[23]](#cite_note-23) and by 1982, generated a revenue of $2 billion (equivalent to $6.67 billion in 2025) in [quarters](/wiki/Quarter_(United_States_coin) "Quarter (United States coin)").[[24]](#cite_note-CBC-1982-24)[[25]](#cite_note-Executive-25)

*Space Invaders* was soon licensed for the [Atari VCS](/wiki/Atari_2600 "Atari 2600") (later known as Atari 2600), becoming the first "[killer app](/wiki/Killer_app "Killer app")" and quadrupling the console's sales.[[26]](#cite_note-RG-41-26) The success of the Atari 2600 in turn revived the home video game market during the second generation of consoles, until the [video game crash of 1983](/wiki/Video_game_crash_of_1983 "Video game crash of 1983").[[27]](#cite_note-27) By the end of the 1970s, the [personal computer game](/wiki/Personal_computer_game "Personal computer game") industry began forming from a hobby culture.

### 1980s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_industry&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: 1980s")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/82/NES-Console-Set.jpg/250px-NES-Console-Set.jpg)](/wiki/File:NES-Console-Set.jpg)

The [Nintendo Entertainment System](/wiki/Nintendo_Entertainment_System "Nintendo Entertainment System"), released in 1985, revived the American video game industry after the video game crash of 1983.

See also: [Golden age of video arcade games](/wiki/Golden_age_of_video_arcade_games "Golden age of video arcade games"), [Second generation of video game consoles](/wiki/Second_generation_of_video_game_consoles "Second generation of video game consoles"), [Video game crash of 1983](/wiki/Video_game_crash_of_1983 "Video game crash of 1983"), [Third generation of video game consoles](/wiki/Third_generation_of_video_game_consoles "Third generation of video game consoles"), and [Fourth generation of video game consoles](/wiki/Fourth_generation_of_video_game_consoles "Fourth generation of video game consoles")

In the early 1980s, the [golden age of video arcade games](/wiki/Golden_age_of_video_arcade_games "Golden age of video arcade games") reached its zenith. The total sales of [arcade video game](/wiki/Arcade_video_game "Arcade video game") machines in North America increased significantly during this period, from $50 million in 1978 to $900 million by 1981,[[28]](#cite_note-Wolf-105-28) with the arcade video game industry's revenue in North America tripling to $2.8 billion in 1980.[[29]](#cite_note-29) By 1981, the arcade video game industry was generating an annual North American revenue of $5 billion[[21]](#cite_note-Whittaker-122-21)[[30]](#cite_note-30) (equivalent to $17.7 billion in 2025). In 1982, the coin-operated video game industry reached its peak, generating $8 billion in quarters,[[31]](#cite_note-Rogers-Larsen-263-31) surpassing the annual gross revenue of both [pop music](/wiki/Pop_music "Pop music") ($4 billion) and [Hollywood](/wiki/Cinema_of_the_United_States "Cinema of the United States") films ($3 billion) combined.[[31]](#cite_note-Rogers-Larsen-263-31) This was also nearly twice as much as the $3.8 billion generated by the home video game industry that year; both the arcade and home video game markets combined in 1982 total of $11.8 billion[[31]](#cite_note-Rogers-Larsen-263-31) (equivalent to $39.4 billion in 2025). The arcade video game industry would continue to generate an annual revenue of $5 billion in quarters through to 1985.[[32]](#cite_note-32) The most successful game of this era was [Namco](/wiki/Namco "Namco")'s *[Pac-Man](/wiki/Pac-Man "Pac-Man")*, released in 1980, of which more than 350,000 cabinets were eventually sold,[[33]](#cite_note-33) and within a year, collected more than $1 billion in quarters;[[34]](#cite_note-Wolf-73-34) in total, *Pac-Man* is estimated to have grossed over 10 billion quarters ($2.5 billion) during the 20th century.[[34]](#cite_note-Wolf-73-34)[[35]](#cite_note-35)

In the early 1980s, [8-bit](/wiki/8-bit "8-bit") home computing and [home-made games](/wiki/Homebrew_(video_games) "Homebrew (video games)") boomed. This was especially in Europe (with the [ZX Spectrum](/wiki/ZX_Spectrum "ZX Spectrum") and [Commodore 64](/wiki/Commodore_64 "Commodore 64")) and in Asia (with the [NEC PC-88](/wiki/NEC_PC-8801 "NEC PC-8801") and [MSX](/wiki/MSX "MSX")). [Video game journalism](/wiki/Video_game_journalism "Video game journalism") arose at that time, which was later expanded to include [covermounted](/wiki/Covermount "Covermount") cassettes and CDs. In 1983, the [North American industry crashed](/wiki/North_American_video_game_crash_of_1983 "North American video game crash of 1983") due to the production of too many badly developed games (quantity over quality), resulting in the fall of the North American industry. The industry would eventually be revitalized by the release of the [Nintendo Entertainment System](/wiki/Nintendo_Entertainment_System "Nintendo Entertainment System"), which resulted in the home console market being dominated by Japanese companies such as [Nintendo](/wiki/Nintendo "Nintendo"),[[10]](#cite_note-newmedia-10) while a professional European video game industry also began taking shape with companies such as [Ocean Software](/wiki/Ocean_Software "Ocean Software") and [Gremlin Interactive](/wiki/Gremlin_Interactive "Gremlin Interactive").[[36]](#cite_note-36) In 1987, Nintendo lost a legal challenge against [Blockbuster Entertainment](/wiki/Blockbuster_Entertainment "Blockbuster Entertainment"), which continued game rentals in the same way as movies. In 1989, the [Game Boy](/wiki/Game_Boy "Game Boy") handheld system was launched.

Video games transitioned from having been showcased at general trade shows like [Consumer Electronics Show](/wiki/Consumer_Electronics_Show "Consumer Electronics Show"), to dedicated shows like [Nintendo Space World](/wiki/Nintendo_Space_World "Nintendo Space World") and [Electronic Entertainment Expo](/wiki/Electronic_Entertainment_Expo "Electronic Entertainment Expo").

### 1990s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_industry&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: 1990s")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/9/9e/PlayStation-SCPH-1000-with-Controller.png/250px-PlayStation-SCPH-1000-with-Controller.png)](/wiki/File:PlayStation-SCPH-1000-with-Controller.png)

The [PlayStation](/wiki/PlayStation_(console) "PlayStation (console)") was the most popular video game console of the fifth generation, with the mass adoption of CD media.

See also: [Fourth generation of video game consoles](/wiki/Fourth_generation_of_video_game_consoles "Fourth generation of video game consoles"), [Fifth generation of video game consoles](/wiki/Fifth_generation_of_video_game_consoles "Fifth generation of video game consoles"), and [Sixth generation of video game consoles](/wiki/Sixth_generation_of_video_game_consoles "Sixth generation of video game consoles")

Game related technology advances of the 1990s include these:

* The "3D Revolution" where [3D polygon graphics](/wiki/3D_computer_graphics "3D computer graphics") became the *[de facto](/wiki/De_facto "De facto")* standard for video game visual presentation, initially in the arcades during the early 1990s,[[37]](#cite_note-Williams-37) and then on home systems with 3D consoles and PC [graphics cards](/wiki/Graphics_processing_unit "Graphics processing unit") in the mid-1990s.
* The widespread adoption of [CD](/wiki/Compact_disc "Compact disc")-based storage and software distribution
* Continuing advancement of [CPU](/wiki/CPU "CPU") speed and sophistication
* Widespread adoption of [GUI](/wiki/Graphical_user_interface "Graphical user interface")-based [operating systems](/wiki/Operating_system "Operating system"), such as the series of [Amiga OS](/wiki/Amiga_OS "Amiga OS"), [Microsoft Windows](/wiki/Microsoft_Windows "Microsoft Windows") and [Mac OS](/wiki/Mac_OS "Mac OS")
* Shrinking of hardware, with [handheld game consoles](/wiki/Handheld_game_consoles "Handheld game consoles") and mobile phones, which enabled [mobile gaming](/wiki/Mobile_gaming "Mobile gaming")
* The emergence of the [Internet](/wiki/Internet "Internet"), which in the late 1990s enabled online cooperative play and [competitive gaming](/wiki/Competitive_gaming "Competitive gaming")

Aside from technology, in the early part of the decade, [licensed](/wiki/Brand_licensing "Brand licensing") games became more popular,[[38]](#cite_note-38)[[39]](#cite_note-39) as did video game sequels.[[40]](#cite_note-40)

The arcades experienced a renaissance in the early 1990s following the release of *[Street Fighter II](/wiki/Street_Fighter_II "Street Fighter II")* (1991), which led to a number of other popular fighting games such as *[Fatal Fury](/wiki/Fatal_Fury "Fatal Fury")* (1991) and *[Mortal Kombat](/wiki/Mortal_Kombat "Mortal Kombat")* (1992).[[41]](#cite_note-41)[[42]](#cite_note-42) The arcade resurgence was further driven by increasing realism,[[43]](#cite_note-GW-43) with the "3D Revolution" from [2D](/wiki/2D_graphics "2D graphics") and [pseudo-3D](/wiki/Pseudo-3D "Pseudo-3D") graphics to true [real-time](/wiki/Real-time_computer_graphics "Real-time computer graphics") [3D polygon graphics](/wiki/3D_computer_graphics "3D computer graphics"), following the release of games such as *[Virtua Racing](/wiki/Virtua_Racing "Virtua Racing")* (1992) and *[Virtua Fighter](/wiki/Virtua_Fighter "Virtua Fighter")* (1993).[[37]](#cite_note-Williams-37)[[44]](#cite_note-tao2-44) In the late 1990s, there was a transition away from arcades to home systems. Until about 1996–1997, arcade video games represented the largest sector of the global video game industry, before arcades declined and the console market surpassed arcade video games for the first time around 1997–1998.[[45]](#cite_note-vgmarket-45) Arcade systems such as the [Sega Model 3](/wiki/Sega_Model_3 "Sega Model 3") remained more technologically advanced than home systems in the late 1990s,[[46]](#cite_note-VF3-46)[[47]](#cite_note-thg-47) but the gap between arcade and home systems began narrowing in the late 1990s.

The video game industry generated worldwide sales of $19.8 billion in 1993[[48]](#cite_note-yearbook_123a-48) (equivalent to $44.1 billion in 2025), $20.8 billion in 1994[[48]](#cite_note-yearbook_123a-48) (equivalent to $45.2 billion in 2025), and an estimated $30 billion in 1998[[49]](#cite_note-49) (equivalent to $59.3 billion in 2025). In the United States alone, in 1994, arcades generated $7 billion[[50]](#cite_note-business_1994-50) in quarters while home console game sales generated $6 billion[[50]](#cite_note-business_1994-50) Combined, this was nearly two and a half times the $5 billion revenue generated by movies in the United States at the time.[[50]](#cite_note-business_1994-50)

### 2000s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_industry&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: 2000s")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/58/PS2-Fat-Console-Set.png/250px-PS2-Fat-Console-Set.png)](/wiki/File:PS2-Fat-Console-Set.png)

The sixth-generation [PlayStation 2](/wiki/PlayStation_2 "PlayStation 2") is the [best-selling console of all time](/wiki/List_of_best-selling_game_consoles "List of best-selling game consoles").

See also: [Sixth generation of video game consoles](/wiki/Sixth_generation_of_video_game_consoles "Sixth generation of video game consoles") and [Seventh generation of video game consoles](/wiki/Seventh_generation_of_video_game_consoles "Seventh generation of video game consoles")

In the 2000s, the video game industry was in heavy development; profit still drove technological advancement used by other industry sectors. Technologies such as [Smartphones](/wiki/Smartphones "Smartphones"), [virtual reality](/wiki/Virtual_reality "Virtual reality"), and [augmented reality](/wiki/Augmented_reality "Augmented reality") were major drivers for game hardware and gameplay development. Though maturing, the video game industry was still very volatile, with third-party video game developers quickly cropping up, and just as quickly, going out of business.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Nevertheless, many [casual games](/wiki/Casual_game "Casual game") and [indie games](/wiki/Indie_game "Indie game") became successful, such as *[Braid](/wiki/Braid_(video_game) "Braid (video game)")* and *[Limbo](/wiki/Limbo_(video_game) "Limbo (video game)")*. Game development for mobile phones (such as [iOS](/wiki/IOS "IOS") and [Android](/wiki/Android_(operating_system) "Android (operating system)") devices) and social networking sites emerged. For example, a [Facebook](/wiki/Facebook "Facebook") game developer, [Zynga](/wiki/Zynga "Zynga"), raised more than $300 million.[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*][[51]](#cite_note-51)

### 2010s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_industry&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: 2010s")]

See also: [Eighth generation of video game consoles](/wiki/Eighth_generation_of_video_game_consoles "Eighth generation of video game consoles")

[Indie games](/wiki/Indie_game "Indie game") are not the main driver but significantly impact the industry, such as *[Spelunky](/wiki/Spelunky "Spelunky")*, *[Fez](/wiki/Fez_(video_game) "Fez (video game)")*, *[Don't Starve](/wiki/Don%27t_Starve "Don't Starve")*, *[Castle Crashers](/wiki/Castle_Crashers "Castle Crashers")*, and *[Minecraft](/wiki/Minecraft "Minecraft")*, with millions of [dollars](/wiki/United_States_dollar "United States dollar") and users.[[52]](#cite_note-entrepreneurial-insights.com-52)[*[unreliable source?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Reliable_sources "Wikipedia:Reliable sources")*] In the 2010s, the shift increased to casual and [mobile gaming](/wiki/Mobile_gaming "Mobile gaming"), and in 2016, the mobile video game market was estimated at $38 billion in revenues, compared to $6 billion for the console market and $33 billion for personal computing gaming.[[53]](#cite_note-53) [Virtual reality](/wiki/Virtual_reality "Virtual reality") and [augmented reality](/wiki/Augmented_reality "Augmented reality") games arose during this decade. As of 2014, newer game companies arose that vertically integrate live operations and publishing such as crowdfunding and other direct-to-consumer efforts, rather than relying on a traditional publishers, and some of these grew substantially.[[54]](#cite_note-54) Spurred by some initial events in the late 2000s, [eSports](/wiki/ESports "ESports") centered around professional players in organized competitions and leagues for prize money, grew greatly over this decade, drawing hundreds of millions of viewers and reaching nearly $500 million in revenue by 2016 and expected to break $1 billion by 2019.[[55]](#cite_note-55)

### 2020s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_industry&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: 2020s")]

See also: [Ninth generation of video game consoles](/wiki/Ninth_generation_of_video_game_consoles "Ninth generation of video game consoles") and [Impact of the COVID-19 pandemic on the video game industry](/wiki/Impact_of_the_COVID-19_pandemic_on_the_video_game_industry "Impact of the COVID-19 pandemic on the video game industry")

The next generations of [Xbox Series X/S](/wiki/Xbox_Series_X/S "Xbox Series X/S") and [PlayStation 5](/wiki/PlayStation_5 "PlayStation 5") were planned for 2020, but the video game industry was affected by the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic") that had a worldwide impact starting in March 2020 due to forced stay-at-home orders by governmental regulations. There were similar impacts to the video game industry as with other industries, such as cancellation of in-person trade shows, conventions and esports events, and the delay of many games into late 2020, 2021, or beyond, and the industry was one of the few to actually thrive from a home-bound population using video games to cope. The market had a 20% year-to-year growth from 2019, reaching over $179 billion in global revenue in both hardware and software for 2020.[[56]](#cite_note-56) Easily learned games with high social interactions were popular, including *[Animal Crossing: New Horizons](/wiki/Animal_Crossing:_New_Horizons "Animal Crossing: New Horizons")*,[[57]](#cite_note-57) *[Fall Guys](/wiki/Fall_Guys "Fall Guys")*, and *[Among Us](/wiki/Among_Us "Among Us")*.[[58]](#cite_note-improb-58)[[59]](#cite_note-59)[[60]](#cite_note-60)[[61]](#cite_note-61)

As the pandemic wore on from 2020 into 2021, a secondary effect was the impact of the [global semiconductor chip shortage](/wiki/2020%E2%80%9321_global_chip_shortage "2020–21 global chip shortage") on hardware manufacturing. The three major console vendors, Nintendo, Microsoft, and Sony, were impacted by availability of supply of core components, and for the latter two, limited the launch of their new consoles. The chip supply shortage also affected personal computer gamers, coupled with demand for computer parts to be used in [cryptocurrency mining](/wiki/Cryptocurrency_mining "Cryptocurrency mining"), which artificially raised prices and made it difficult to purchase newer components.[[62]](#cite_note-62) However, after cryptocurrency mining started paying out less during and following the [2021–2022 cryptocurrency crash](/wiki/Cryptocurrency_bubble "Cryptocurrency bubble"), computer parts such as [GPUs](/wiki/Graphics_processing_unit "Graphics processing unit") have become more affordable as of August 2022.[[63]](#cite_note-63)

## Economics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_industry&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Economics")]

BillionsUSDYear03060901201501971197819851992199920062013Virtual RealityMobilePersonal ComputersHandheldsConsolesArcadeGlobal revenue estimates of the video game i...

View [source data](https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/Data:Global_revenue_estimates_of_the_video_game_industry_from_1971_to_2018,_not_adjusted_for_inflation.tab "c:Data:Global revenue estimates of the video game industry from 1971 to 2018, not adjusted for inflation.tab").

Global revenue estimates of the video game industry from 1971 to 2018, not adjusted for inflation, according to market research firm Pelham Smithers.[[45]](#cite_note-vgmarket-45)   
 The chart shows the impacts of the [1977 crash](/wiki/Video_game_crash_of_1977 "Video game crash of 1977"), the [golden age of arcade games](/wiki/Golden_age_of_arcade_games "Golden age of arcade games") (1978–1983), the [video game crash of 1983](/wiki/Video_game_crash_of_1983 "Video game crash of 1983"), the [console revival](/wiki/Third_generation_of_video_game_consoles "Third generation of video game consoles") (late 1980s), and the rise of [mobile gaming](/wiki/Mobile_gaming "Mobile gaming") since 2008.

Early development costs were minimal, and video games could be quite profitable. Games developed by a single programmer, or by a small team of programmers and artists, could sell hundreds of thousands of copies each. Many of these games only took a few months to create, so developers could release multiple games per year. Thus, publishers could often be generous with benefits, such as [royalties](/wiki/Royalties "Royalties") on the games sold. Many early game publishers started from this economic climate, such as [Origin Systems](/wiki/Origin_Systems "Origin Systems"), [Sierra Entertainment](/wiki/Sierra_Entertainment "Sierra Entertainment"), [Capcom](/wiki/Capcom "Capcom"), [Activision](/wiki/Activision "Activision") and [Electronic Arts](/wiki/Electronic_Arts "Electronic Arts").

As computing and graphics power increased, so too did the size of development teams, as larger staffs were needed to address the ever-increasing technical and design complexities. The larger teams consist of programmers, artists, game designers, and producers. Their salaries can range anywhere from $50,000 to $120,000 generating large labor costs for firms producing video games[[64]](#cite_note-64) which can often take between one and three years to develop. Modern budgets typically reach millions of dollars and use [middleware](/wiki/Game_Middleware "Game Middleware") and pre-built [game engines](/wiki/Game_engine "Game engine"). In addition to growing development costs, marketing budgets have grown dramatically, sometimes two to three times of the cost of development.[[65]](#cite_note-65)

Traditionally, the [video game monetization](/wiki/Video_game_monetization "Video game monetization") method is to sell hard copies in retail store. Cheaper production and distribution methods include [online distribution](/wiki/Digital_distribution "Digital distribution").[[66]](#cite_note-66)

In the 2010s, the video game industry had a major impact on the economy through the sales of major systems and games such as *[Call of Duty: Black Ops](/wiki/Call_of_Duty:_Black_Ops "Call of Duty: Black Ops")*, which had over $650 million of sales in the game's first five days and which set a five-day global record for a movie, book or video game.[[67]](#cite_note-67) The game's income was more than the opening weekend of *[Spider-Man 3](/wiki/Spider-Man_3 "Spider-Man 3")* and the previous title holder for a video game *[Halo 3](/wiki/Halo_3 "Halo 3")*.[[68]](#cite_note-68) Many individuals have also benefited from the economic success of video games including the former chairman of [Nintendo](/wiki/Nintendo "Nintendo") and Japan's third richest man: [Hiroshi Yamauchi](/wiki/Hiroshi_Yamauchi "Hiroshi Yamauchi").[[69]](#cite_note-69) By 2014, the global video game market was valued at over $93 billion.[[70]](#cite_note-70)

The industry wide adoption of high-definition graphics during the seventh generation of consoles greatly increased development teams' sizes and reduced the number of [high-budget, high-quality games](/wiki/Triple_A_(gaming) "Triple A (gaming)") under development. In 2013 [Richard Hilleman](/wiki/Richard_Hilleman "Richard Hilleman") of Electronic Arts estimated that only 25 developers were working on such games for the eighth console generation, compared to 125 at the same point in the seventh generation-console cycle seven or eight years earlier.[[71]](#cite_note-71)

By 2018, the United States video game industry had matched that of the United States film industry on basis of revenue, with both having made around US$43 billion that year.[[72]](#cite_note-72)[[73]](#cite_note-73)

Since 2000, the video game industry was considered [recession](/wiki/Recession "Recession")-proof, having thrived compared to other industries during the 2008 [Great Recession](/wiki/Great_Recession "Great Recession"), and as one of the more profitable industries during the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic") in 2020 and 2021. Video games are seen as a low-cost vice and entertainment for consumers when approaching recession.[[74]](#cite_note-74)[[75]](#cite_note-75) However, in 2022, atop pandemic economic fallout including chip shortages, supply chain disruption, and consumers preferring outdoor activities, the industry started to indicate recession with global revenues falling for the first time in twenty years.[[76]](#cite_note-76)

### Retail

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_industry&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Retail")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2d/Isokatu_28_Oulu_20190210.jpg/250px-Isokatu_28_Oulu_20190210.jpg)](/wiki/File:Isokatu_28_Oulu_20190210.jpg)

[GameStop](/wiki/GameStop "GameStop") video game store at the Isokatu street in [Oulu](/wiki/Oulu "Oulu")

The industry's shift from [brick and mortar](/wiki/Brick_and_mortar "Brick and mortar") retail to digital downloads led to a severe sales decline at video game retailers such as [GameStop](/wiki/GameStop "GameStop"), following other media retailers superseded by Internet delivery, such as [Blockbuster](/wiki/Blockbuster_LLC "Blockbuster LLC"), [Tower Records](/wiki/Tower_Records_(music_retailer) "Tower Records (music retailer)"), and [Virgin Megastores](/wiki/Virgin_Megastores "Virgin Megastores"). GameStop diversified its services by purchasing chains that repair wireless devices and expanding its trade-in program through which customers trade used games for credit towards new games.[[77]](#cite_note-77) The company began to produce its own merchandise and games. In Britain, the games retailer [Game](/wiki/Game_(retailer) "Game (retailer)") revamped its stores so customers would spend time playing games there. It built a gaming arena for events and tournaments.[[78]](#cite_note-78) The shift to digital marketplaces, especially for smartphones, led to an influx of inexpensive and disposable games,[[79]](#cite_note-79) and lower engagement among gamers who otherwise purchased new games from retail.[[80]](#cite_note-80) Customers also shifted away from the tradition of buying games on their first day of release.[[81]](#cite_note-81)

Publishers often funded trade-in deals to encourage consumers to purchase new games. Trade-in customers at the Australian retailer Game would purchase twice the games per year as non-trade-in customers. The sale of pre-owned games kept retailers in business, and composed about a third of Game's revenue. Retailers also saved on the UK's value-added tax, which only taxed the retailer's profit on pre-owned games, rather than the full sale on regular games. The former trade-in retail executives behind the trade-in price comparison site Trade In Detectives estimated that the United Kingdom's trade-in industry was about a third of the size of its new games business.[[82]](#cite_note-Eurogamer:_trade-in_myths-82) They figured that sites such as [eBay](/wiki/EBay "EBay"), which convert used games into cash, compose about a quarter of the UK's trade-in market,[[83]](#cite_note-83) but do not keep the credit within the industry. While consumers might appear to receive better offers on these sites, they also take about 15 percent of the selling price in fees. Alternatively, some retailers will match the trade-in values offered by their competitors. Microsoft's original plan for the Xbox One attempted to translate trade-in deals for the digital marketplace, with a database of product licenses that shops would be able to resell with publisher permission, though the plan was poorly received or poorly sold.[[82]](#cite_note-Eurogamer:_trade-in_myths-82)

## Practices

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_industry&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Practices")]

Video game industry practices are similar to those of other entertainment industries (e.g., the [music recording industry](/wiki/Recording_industry "Recording industry")), but the video game industry in particular has been accused of treating its development talent poorly. This promotes independent development, as developers leave to form new companies and projects. In some notable cases, these new companies grow large and impersonal, having adopted the business practices of their forebears, and ultimately perpetuate the cycle.

However, unlike the music industry, where modern technology has allowed a fully professional product to be created extremely inexpensively by an independent musician, modern games require increasing amounts of manpower and equipment. This dynamic makes [publishers](/wiki/Video_game_publisher "Video game publisher"), who fund the [developers](/wiki/Video_game_developer "Video game developer"), much more important than in the music industry.

### Breakaways

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_industry&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Breakaways")]

In the video game industry, it is common for developers to leave their current studio and start their own. A particularly famous case is the "original" independent developer [Activision](/wiki/Activision "Activision"), founded by former [Atari](/wiki/Atari "Atari") developers. Activision grew to become the world's second largest game publisher.[[84]](#cite_note-84) In the meantime, many of the original developers left to work on other projects. For example, founder [Alan Miller](/wiki/Alan_Miller_(game_designer) "Alan Miller (game designer)") left Activision to start another video game development company, [Accolade](/wiki/Accolade,_Inc. "Accolade, Inc.") (now [Atari](/wiki/Atari "Atari") née [Infogrames](/wiki/Infogrames "Infogrames")).

Activision was popular among developers for giving them credit in the packaging and title screens for their games, while Atari disallowed this practice. As the video game industry took off in the mid-1980s, many developers faced the more distressing problem of working with fly-by-night or unscrupulous publishers that would either fold unexpectedly or run off with the game profits.

### Piracy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_industry&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Piracy")]

The industry claims [software piracy](/wiki/Software_piracy "Software piracy") to be a big problem, and takes measures to counter this.[[85]](#cite_note-85)
[Digital rights management](/wiki/Digital_rights_management "Digital rights management") have proved to be the most unpopular with gamers, as a measure to counter piracy.[[86]](#cite_note-86)
The most popular and effective strategy to counter piracy is to change the business model to [freemium](/wiki/Freemium "Freemium"), where gamers pay for their in-game needs or service. Strong server-side security is required for this, to properly distinguish authentic transactions from [hacked](/wiki/Cheating_in_video_games "Cheating in video games") transactions.

### Creative control

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_industry&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Creative control")]

On various Internet forums, some gamers have expressed disapproval of publishers having creative control since publishers are more apt to follow short-term market trends rather than invest in risky but potentially lucrative ideas. On the other hand, publishers may know better than developers what consumers want. The relationship between video game developers and publishers parallels the relationship between [recording artists](/wiki/Recording_Artists%27_Coalition "Recording Artists' Coalition") and [record labels](/wiki/RIAA "RIAA") in many ways. But unlike the [music industry](/wiki/Music_industry "Music industry"), which has seen flat or declining sales in the early 2000s,[[87]](#cite_note-87)[[88]](#cite_note-88)[[89]](#cite_note-89) the video game industry continues to grow.[[90]](#cite_note-90)

In the [computer games](/wiki/Computer_game "Computer game") industry, it is easier to create a startup, resulting in many successful companies. The [console](/wiki/Video_game_console "Video game console") industry is more closed, and a game developer must have up to three licenses from the [console manufacturer](/wiki/Console_manufacturer "Console manufacturer"):

1. A license to develop games for the console
2. The publisher must have a license to publish games for the console
3. A separate license for each game

In addition, the developer must usually buy development systems from the console manufacturer in order to develop a game for consideration, as well as obtain concept approval for the game from the console manufacturer. Therefore, the developer normally has to have a publishing deal in place before starting development on a game project, but in order to secure a publishing deal, the developer must have a track record of console development, something which few startups will have.

### Alternatives

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_industry&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Alternatives")]

There are some alternative methods for publishing video games, such as self-publish using the [shareware](/wiki/Shareware "Shareware") or [open source](/wiki/Open-source_software "Open-source software") model over the [Internet](/wiki/Internet "Internet") and Cloud gaming.

#### Cloud gaming

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_industry&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Cloud gaming")]

See also: [Cloud gaming](/wiki/Cloud_gaming "Cloud gaming")

Cloud gaming is a gaming method based on cloud computing. The main technologies used in cloud gaming include cloud computing technology, which completes game operation and screen rendering in the cloud, and streaming media transmission technology between the player's terminal and the cloud.[[91]](#cite_note-:1-91)

Cloud gaming, also known as on-demand gaming, is a new technology based on cloud computing. Obviously, on-demand gaming means that if you want it, you can get it; it's a service that can improve game performance.[[92]](#cite_note-:2-92)

Cloud computing technology includes two categories: narrow and broad. Narrow cloud computing refers to the delivery and usage model of IT infrastructure, which means obtaining the required resources in an on-demand and easily scalable manner through the network; broad cloud computing refers to the delivery and usage model of services, which means obtaining the required services in an on-demand and easily scalable manner through the network. Such services can be IT and software, Internet-related, or other services.[[91]](#cite_note-:1-91)

Cloud gaming is similar to video on demand. Many providers offer this technology, but it only requires a certain network speed and a monthly rental fee.[[93]](#cite_note-:3-93)

People are becoming increasingly familiar with the term "cloud computing" lately, and many are paying attention to its various applications, such as cloud IoT, cloud identity, cloud storage, and cloud security. The "cloud" has changed people's perception of the internet. It's worth noting that in this era of booming gaming, people are often limited by low-spec devices, which is very disappointing for gamers. Therefore, game developers are turning their attention to cloud gaming, which combines cloud computing with gaming.[[92]](#cite_note-:2-92)

In the cloud gaming model, all game logic and rendering run on the server side, and then the compressed video is transmitted from the server to the user.[[91]](#cite_note-:1-91) This way, players don't need a computer with a high-end CPU and GPU; the only requirements are basic video decompression software and a reliable network. However, if the network is unstable, cloud gaming can be unreliable.[[94]](#cite_note-:4-94)

Cloud gaming eliminates the dependence on hardware. For servers, it is only necessary to improve server performance without developing new host machines; for users, they can get higher image quality without buying high-performance computers. In other words, they can spend a small amount of money to rent a better computer to play various games, just like watching TV with a set-top box. This means that a lot of money can be saved on computer hardware, especially GPUs.[[91]](#cite_note-:1-91)

By performing computation and rendering on remote servers, cloud gaming users can enjoy advanced games on low-cost devices via network connection without high-performance hardware.[[91]](#cite_note-:1-91)[[93]](#cite_note-:3-93) This technology is increasingly seen as a natural extension of cloud services and is considered one of the key trends for the future development of the gaming industry.[[91]](#cite_note-:1-91)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5b/Google_Stadia_logo.svg/250px-Google_Stadia_logo.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Google_Stadia_logo.svg)

[Google Stadia](/wiki/Google_Stadia "Google Stadia"), a cloud gaming service published by Google, was shut down on January 18, 2023

With the development of 5G technology and advancements in video compression, the network latency and stability requirements of cloud gaming are becoming easier to meet.[[94]](#cite_note-:4-94) In 2024, the global cloud gaming industry reached $2.27 billion, and it is projected to exceed $21 billion by 2030, representing a compound annual growth rate of over 44%.[[95]](#cite_note-95) However, Although network and cloud rendering technologies developed significantly, user experience remains highly dependent on low network latency and high network stability. Poor network connectivity will continue to impact game performance and the player's gaming experience.[[94]](#cite_note-:4-94)

Compared with traditional game modes, cloud gaming can greatly reduce the equipment costs for players to play games. For many high-quality games that require long-term updates, cloud gaming can also reduce the cost for game developers to release and update and maintain games.

However, in terms of ensuring the player's gaming experience, cloud gaming has certain gaps compared with traditional games, mainly including:

* The game interaction latency depends on the network communication latency. Compared with traditional online games, which only need to transmit game state data, cloud gaming's multimedia transmission is more sensitive to network latency. When the network communication quality is poor, players will directly feel the high latency between command input and screen update, thus significantly reducing the quality of the player's gaming experience.[[91]](#cite_note-:1-91)[[94]](#cite_note-:4-94)
* The quality of the multimedia stream rendering of the game scene depends on the network communication bandwidth. Compared with traditional online games, cloud gaming's multimedia stream requires more bandwidth, and the higher the quality of the multimedia stream, the higher the bandwidth resources it consumes.[[91]](#cite_note-:1-91)[[93]](#cite_note-:3-93)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_industry&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Regional distribution")]

As with other forms of media, video games have often been released in different world regions at different times.[[97]](#cite_note-denofgeek1-97) The practice has been used where [localization](/wiki/Video_game_localization "Video game localization") is not done in parallel with the rest of development[[98]](#cite_note-98) or where the game must be encoded differently, as in [PAL](/wiki/PAL "PAL") vs. [NTSC](/wiki/NTSC "NTSC").[[99]](#cite_note-99) It has also been used to provide price discrimination in different markets or to focus limited marketing resources.[[97]](#cite_note-denofgeek1-97) Developers may also stagger digital releases so as not to overwhelm the servers hosting the game.[[100]](#cite_note-100)

## International practices

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_industry&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: International practices")]

The video game industry had its primary roots in the United States following the introduction of arcade games and console systems, with Japan soon following. With the introduction of the personal computer, Western Europe also became a major center for video game development. Since then, the industry is primarily led by companies in North America, Europe, and Japan, but other regions, including Australia/New Zealand, and other East Asian countries including China and South Korea, have become significant sectors for the industry.

### World trends

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_industry&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: World trends")]

International video game revenue was over $142B in 2022.[[101]](#cite_note-newzoo2022-101) This is almost double the revenue of the international [film industry](/wiki/Film_industry "Film industry") in 2023.[[102]](#cite_note-102)

The gaming industry saw strong growth in 2020, the first year of the pandemic, and this trend continued into 2021.[[103]](#cite_note-103)

| 10 | [Canada](/wiki/Video_games_in_Canada "Video games in Canada") | 24 million |

In general, spending on gaming tends to increase with increase in nominal GDP. However, gaming is relatively more popular in [East Asia](/wiki/East_Asia "East Asia"), and relatively less popular in [India](/wiki/India "India").
