### North America

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_industry&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: North America")]

#### Canada

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_industry&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Canada")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e3/Ubisoft_Montreal.jpg/250px-Ubisoft_Montreal.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ubisoft_Montreal.jpg)

[Ubisoft Montreal](/wiki/Ubisoft_Montreal "Ubisoft Montreal")

Main article: [Video gaming in Canada](/wiki/Video_gaming_in_Canada "Video gaming in Canada")

Canada has the third largest video game industry in terms of employment numbers.[[106]](#cite_note-networkworld1-106) The video game industry has also been booming in Montreal since 1997, coinciding with the opening of [Ubisoft Montreal](/wiki/Ubisoft_Montreal "Ubisoft Montreal").[[107]](#cite_note-107) Recently, the city has attracted world leading game developers and publishers studios such as [Ubisoft](/wiki/Ubisoft "Ubisoft"), [EA](/wiki/Electronic_Arts "Electronic Arts"), [Eidos Interactive](/wiki/Eidos_Interactive "Eidos Interactive"), [Artificial Mind and Movement](/wiki/Artificial_Mind_and_Movement "Artificial Mind and Movement"), [BioWare](/wiki/BioWare "BioWare"), [Warner Bros. Interactive Entertainment](/wiki/Warner_Bros._Interactive_Entertainment "Warner Bros. Interactive Entertainment"), and [Strategy First](/wiki/Strategy_First "Strategy First"), mainly because video games jobs have been heavily subsidized by the provincial government. Every year, this industry generates billions of dollars and thousands of jobs in the Montreal area.[[108]](#cite_note-108) [Vancouver](/wiki/Vancouver "Vancouver") has also developed a particularly large [cluster](/wiki/Business_cluster "Business cluster") of [video game](/wiki/Video_game "Video game") developers, the largest of which, [Electronic Arts](/wiki/Electronic_Arts "Electronic Arts"), employs over two thousand people. The *[Assassin's Creed](/wiki/Assassin%27s_Creed "Assassin's Creed")* series, along with the *[Tom Clancy](/wiki/Tom_Clancy "Tom Clancy")* series have all been produced in Canada and have achieved worldwide success. For consumers, the largest video games convention in Canada is the Enthusiast Gaming Live Expo (EGLX).[[109]](#cite_note-109)

#### United States

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_industry&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: United States")]

Main article: [Video gaming in the United States](/wiki/Video_gaming_in_the_United_States "Video gaming in the United States")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/43/Activisionheadquarters.jpg/250px-Activisionheadquarters.jpg)](/wiki/File:Activisionheadquarters.jpg)

Activision (now [Activision Blizzard](/wiki/Activision_Blizzard "Activision Blizzard")) is the first independent or third-party video game developer.

The video game industry got its start in the United States in the 1970s and early 1980s with the creation of arcade games like *[Pong](/wiki/Pong "Pong")* and the first home console, the [Magnavox Odyssey](/wiki/Magnavox_Odyssey "Magnavox Odyssey"). Several factors, including loss of publishing control, a flooded market, and competition from personal computers, led to the [1983 video game crash](/wiki/1983_video_game_crash "1983 video game crash") in the U.S., affecting both arcades and home game systems. Nintendo's introduction of the Nintendo Entertainment System helped to revitalize the industry, but until Microsoft's introduction of the [Xbox](/wiki/Xbox "Xbox") in the early 2000s, the hardware side was dominated by mostly Japanese-developed systems. Instead, much of the industry's growth in the U.S. was on game development, implementing new game technologies and gameplay concepts, as well as creating the large-scale publisher model used by companies like [Electronic Arts](/wiki/Electronic_Arts "Electronic Arts") to support marketing and distribution of games.

The United States has the largest video games presence in the world in terms of total industry employees.[[106]](#cite_note-networkworld1-106)[[110]](#cite_note-110) In 2017, the U.S. game industry as a whole was worth US$18.4 billion and consisted of roughly 2457 companies that had a rough total of 220,000 people employed.[[111]](#cite_note-Takahashi_2017-111)[[112]](#cite_note-112) U.S. video game revenue is forecast to reach $230 billion by 2022,[[113]](#cite_note-113) making it the largest video game market in the world.[[114]](#cite_note-newzoo2014-114) Over 150 million Americans play video games, with an average age of 35 and a gender breakdown of 59 percent male and 41 percent female.[[115]](#cite_note-115) American gamers are more likely to vote than non-gamers, feel that the economy is the most important political issue, and lean [conservative](/wiki/Conservative "Conservative"), however party demographics are split evenly with 38% identifying as Democrats, 38% identifying as Republicans, and 24% identifying as Independents.[[116]](#cite_note-116)

### Europe

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_industry&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Europe")]

Main articles: [Video gaming in Russia](/wiki/Video_gaming_in_Russia "Video gaming in Russia"), [Video gaming in France](/wiki/Video_gaming_in_France "Video gaming in France"), [Video gaming in Italy](/wiki/Video_gaming_in_Italy "Video gaming in Italy"), and [Video gaming in Spain](/wiki/Video_gaming_in_Spain "Video gaming in Spain")

#### Germany

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_industry&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Germany")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/78/Gamescom_2009_-_Fujitsu_%285154%29.jpg/250px-Gamescom_2009_-_Fujitsu_%285154%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gamescom_2009_-_Fujitsu_(5154).jpg)

The [Gamescom](/wiki/Gamescom "Gamescom") in [Cologne](/wiki/Cologne "Cologne") is the world's leading game trade fair.

Main article: [Video gaming in Germany](/wiki/Video_gaming_in_Germany "Video gaming in Germany")

[Germany](/wiki/Germany "Germany") has the largest video games market in Europe, with revenues of $4.1 billion forecast for 2017.[[117]](#cite_note-117) The annual [Gamescom](/wiki/Gamescom "Gamescom") in [Cologne](/wiki/Cologne "Cologne") is Europe's largest video game expo.

One of the earliest internationally successful video game companies was [Gütersloh](/wiki/G%C3%BCtersloh "Gütersloh")-based [Rainbow Arts](/wiki/Rainbow_Arts "Rainbow Arts") (founded in 1984) who were responsible for publishing the popular *[Turrican](/wiki/Turrican "Turrican")* series of games. The [Anno series](/wiki/Anno_(series) "Anno (series)") and [The Settlers series](/wiki/The_Settlers "The Settlers") are globally popular strategy game franchises since the 1990s. The [Gothic series](/wiki/Gothic_series "Gothic series"), [SpellForce](/wiki/SpellForce:_The_Order_of_Dawn "SpellForce: The Order of Dawn") and [Risen](/wiki/Risen_(video_game) "Risen (video game)") are established RPG franchises. The [X series](/wiki/X_(game_series) "X (game series)") by [Egosoft](/wiki/Egosoft "Egosoft") is the best-selling space simulation. The [FIFA Manager series](/wiki/FIFA_Manager_(video_game_series) "FIFA Manager (video game series)") was also developed in Germany. The German action game [Spec Ops: The Line](/wiki/Spec_Ops:_The_Line "Spec Ops: The Line") (2012) was successful in the markets and received largely positive reviews. One of the most famed games from Germany is *[Far Cry](/wiki/Far_Cry "Far Cry")* (2004) by [Frankfurt](/wiki/Frankfurt "Frankfurt")-based [Crytek](/wiki/Crytek "Crytek"), who also produced the topseller *[Crysis](/wiki/Crysis "Crysis")* and its sequels later.

Other well-known current and former developers from Germany include [Ascaron](/wiki/Ascaron "Ascaron"), [Blue Byte](/wiki/Blue_Byte "Blue Byte"), [Deck13](/wiki/Deck13 "Deck13"), [EA Phenomic](/wiki/EA_Phenomic "EA Phenomic"), [Piranha Bytes](/wiki/Piranha_Bytes "Piranha Bytes"), [Radon Labs](/wiki/Radon_Labs "Radon Labs"), [Related Designs](/wiki/Related_Designs "Related Designs"), [Spellbound Entertainment](/wiki/Spellbound_Entertainment "Spellbound Entertainment") and [Yager Development](/wiki/Yager_Development "Yager Development"). Publishers include [Deep Silver](/wiki/Deep_Silver "Deep Silver") ([Plaion](/wiki/Plaion "Plaion")), [dtp entertainment](/wiki/Dtp_entertainment "Dtp entertainment"), [Kalypso Media](/wiki/Kalypso_Media "Kalypso Media") and [Nintendo Europe](/wiki/Nintendo_Europe "Nintendo Europe"). [Bigpoint Games](/wiki/Bigpoint_Games "Bigpoint Games"), [Gameforge](/wiki/Gameforge "Gameforge"), [Goodgame Studios](/wiki/Goodgame_Studios "Goodgame Studios") and [Wooga](/wiki/Wooga "Wooga") are among the world's leading browser game and social network game developers/distributors.

#### United Kingdom

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_industry&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: United Kingdom")]

Main article: [Video gaming in the United Kingdom](/wiki/Video_gaming_in_the_United_Kingdom "Video gaming in the United Kingdom")

The [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom")'s video game industry is the third largest in the world in terms of developer success and sales of hardware and software by country alone but fourth behind Canada in terms of people employed.[[106]](#cite_note-networkworld1-106) The size of the UK game industry is comparable to its film or music industries.[[118]](#cite_note-118)

Like most European countries, the UK entered the video game industry through personal computers rather than video game consoles. Low-cost computers like the [ZX Spectrum](/wiki/ZX_Spectrum "ZX Spectrum") and [Amiga 500](/wiki/Amiga_500 "Amiga 500") led to numerous "bedroom coders" that would make and sell games through mail-order or to distributors that helped to mass-produce them.[[119]](#cite_note-independent_origins-119) Coupled with quirky [british humour](/wiki/British_humour "British humour"), the "Britsoft" wave of popular games led to a number of influential people and studios in the 1990s.[[120]](#cite_note-120) As game programming became more complex and costly in the early 2000s, more traditional studio structures arose to support both personal computers and consoles, with several studios that, in some form or another, remain highly regarded and influential in the present.[[121]](#cite_note-bbc_billion_pounds-121)

Some of the studios have become defunct or been purchased by larger companies such as *[LittleBigPlanet](/wiki/LittleBigPlanet "LittleBigPlanet")* developer [Media Molecule](/wiki/Media_Molecule "Media Molecule"),[[122]](#cite_note-122) and [Codemasters](/wiki/Codemasters "Codemasters").[[123]](#cite_note-123) The country is home to some of the world's most successful video game franchises, such as *[Tomb Raider](/wiki/Tomb_Raider "Tomb Raider")*, *[Grand Theft Auto](/wiki/Grand_Theft_Auto "Grand Theft Auto")*, *[Fable](/wiki/Fable_(video_game_series) "Fable (video game series)")*, *[Colin McRae Dirt](/wiki/Colin_McRae:_Dirt "Colin McRae: Dirt")*, and *[Total War](/wiki/Total_War_(video_game_series) "Total War (video game series)")*.

The country also went without tax relief until March 21, 2012[[124]](#cite_note-124) when the British government changed its mind on tax relief for UK developers, which without, meant most of the talented development within the UK may move overseas for more profit, along with parents of certain video game developers which would pay for having games developed in the UK. The industry trade body TIGA estimates that it will increase the games development sector's contribution to UK GDP by £283 million, generate £172 million in new and protected tax receipts to HM Treasury, and could cost just £96 million over five years.[[125]](#cite_note-125) Before the tax relief was introduced there was a fear that the UK game industry could fall behind other leading game industries around the world such as France and Canada, of which Canada overtook the UK in terms of job numbers in the industry in 2010.[[126]](#cite_note-126)

### Africa

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_industry&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Africa")]

The video game industry is still in its infancy throughout the African continent, but due to the continent's young population and increasing technological literacy, the sector is growing rapidly. African countries such as [South Africa](/wiki/Video_gaming_in_South_Africa "Video gaming in South Africa"), [Nigeria](/wiki/Video_gaming_in_Nigeria "Video gaming in Nigeria"), and [Kenya](/wiki/Video_gaming_in_Kenya "Video gaming in Kenya") have been making rapid advances in [mobile game development](/wiki/Mobile_gaming "Mobile gaming"), both within their country and internationally,[[127]](#cite_note-Africa_top_10-127) but due to limited funding and a market overcrowded with Western games, success has thus far been minimal.[[128]](#cite_note-Mail_&_Guardian-128)

### Asia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_industry&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Asia")]

#### Bangladesh

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_industry&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Bangladesh")]

Main article: [Video gaming in Bangladesh](/wiki/Video_gaming_in_Bangladesh "Video gaming in Bangladesh")

Video gaming is a relatively new sector in Bangladesh. Games have been developed since 2002, mostly independently. However, from 2014, some IT companies have started to develop video games commercially. Some research has been carried out at various universities to improve the video game development sector.[[129]](#cite_note-129) In 2020, the first person shooter Zero Hour was released on [Steam](/wiki/Steam_(service) "Steam (service)") in Early Access with the version 1.0 being released on September 9, 2024, and has received positive reviews from gamers. It is the first game from Bangladesh to be released on the platform.[[130]](#cite_note-130)

#### China

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_industry&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: China")]

Main article: [Video gaming in China](/wiki/Video_gaming_in_China "Video gaming in China")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cd/Tencent_Seafront_Tower_in_Dec2020.jpg/250px-Tencent_Seafront_Tower_in_Dec2020.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tencent_Seafront_Tower_in_Dec2020.jpg)

[Tencent](/wiki/Tencent "Tencent") is the largest company in the world by video game revenue.[[131]](#cite_note-131)

China had not been a major factor in the global video game market early on due to economic factors, governmental oversight, and a black market for foreign products. The government initiated a ban on video game consoles in 2000 that lasted through 2014, during which China's video game market grew for personal computer games, particularly subscription-based and microtransaction-based ones that were amenable to use in PC cafes, and later into mobile games. Media publishers like [Tencent](/wiki/Tencent "Tencent") and [NetEase](/wiki/NetEase "NetEase") focused on these types of games, growing successfully during the 2010s to become leading international companies. As of 2015, China's video game market revenue exceeds that of the United States, and is the largest country by both revenue and number of players.[[132]](#cite_note-132)[[133]](#cite_note-cnxb-133) China is also the largest contributor towards [esports](/wiki/Esports "Esports") in both revenue and in the number of professional players from the country.[[134]](#cite_note-134) The industry, like most media in China, is tightly controlled by the government, with strong restrictions on what content may be in games,[[135]](#cite_note-bloomberg_aug2018-135) and incorporation of anti-addiction measures to limit playtime.[[136]](#cite_note-136) It is home to [Asia Game Show](/wiki/Asia_Game_Show "Asia Game Show"), the largest game convention in the world by attendance.[[137]](#cite_note-137)

#### Japan

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_industry&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: Japan")]

Main article: [Video gaming in Japan](/wiki/Video_gaming_in_Japan "Video gaming in Japan")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bb/Nintendo_Headquarters_-_panoramio.jpg/250px-Nintendo_Headquarters_-_panoramio.jpg)](/wiki/File:Nintendo_Headquarters_-_panoramio.jpg)

Headquarters of [Nintendo](/wiki/Nintendo "Nintendo") in [Kyoto](/wiki/Kyoto "Kyoto"), Japan, 2006

The [Japanese](/wiki/Japan "Japan") video game industry is markedly different from the industry in North America, Europe and Australia. Japan initially trailed the United States in entering the video game sector as its companies followed trends set by their American partners, but started to pioneer their own ideas soon after. Several Japanese-developed arcade games, such as *[Space Invaders](/wiki/Space_Invaders "Space Invaders")*, helped to usher in the [golden age of arcade video games](/wiki/Golden_age_of_arcade_video_games "Golden age of arcade video games") from 1978 to 1982. The 1983 video game crash that affected the North American market did have small but short-term effects in Japan, as most companies involved in the business were well-established and could weather the disruption. [Nintendo](/wiki/Nintendo "Nintendo") took the opportunity to push the [Nintendo Entertainment System](/wiki/Nintendo_Entertainment_System "Nintendo Entertainment System"), a rebranding of its Famicom system, into the Western markets after the crash, implementing technical and business practices to avoid the factors that created the 1983 crash but also secured its control on what games were published for the system. Japan became the dominant home for consoles and console games through the early 2000s, challenged only by the incorporation of large publishers in the West and the [Xbox](/wiki/Xbox "Xbox") line of consoles from Microsoft. Nintendo along with companies like [Sega](/wiki/Sega "Sega"), [Sony Interactive Entertainment](/wiki/Sony_Interactive_Entertainment "Sony Interactive Entertainment"), and [Capcom](/wiki/Capcom "Capcom") are dominant leaders in the Japanese video game industry.

Nintendo themselves are recognized for having created some of the most positively-reviewed and best-selling video game series such as *[Mario](/wiki/Mario "Mario")*, *[Donkey Kong](/wiki/Donkey_Kong "Donkey Kong")*, *[The Legend of Zelda](/wiki/The_Legend_of_Zelda "The Legend of Zelda")*, *[Metroid](/wiki/Metroid "Metroid")* and *[Pokémon](/wiki/Pok%C3%A9mon "Pokémon")*.

In recent years, [consoles](/wiki/Video_game_console "Video game console") and arcade games have both been overtaken by downloadable [free-to-play](/wiki/Free-to-play "Free-to-play") games on the [PC](/wiki/PC_game "PC game") and [mobile](/wiki/Mobile_game "Mobile game") platforms.[[138]](#cite_note-economist12-138)[[139]](#cite_note-capcom_marketdata-139)

#### India

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_industry&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: India")]

Main article: [Video gaming in India](/wiki/Video_gaming_in_India "Video gaming in India")

Video gaming in India is an emerging market since India is experiencing strong growth in online gaming, making it one of the top gaming markets in the world. Over the past few decades, the Indian gaming industry has gone from close to nonexistent in the 1990s to one of the top markets globally in the late 2010s. In 2019, the [online gaming](/wiki/Online_game "Online game") market in India was estimated at ₹6,200 [crore](/wiki/Crore "Crore") (US$730 million) with an estimated 300 million gamers, a 41.6% increase from 2018.[[140]](#cite_note-:0-140) As of 2021, it is one of the top five [mobile gaming](/wiki/Mobile_game "Mobile game") markets in the world. The industry is projected to reach 510 million gamers by 2022.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

#### South Korea

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_industry&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: South Korea")]

Main article: [Video gaming in South Korea](/wiki/Video_gaming_in_South_Korea "Video gaming in South Korea")

The video game industry in South Korea generally followed the same early trends as the Japanese market, but players started focusing on massively-multiplayer online games (MMO) and other games that could be played at [PC bangs](/wiki/PC_bang "PC bang") (Internet cafes). South Korea was one of the first major regions involved in [esports](/wiki/Esport "Esport") in the 1990s and 2000s, and today a large number of professional esports players originate from South Korea.

#### Taiwan

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_industry&action=edit&section=39 "Edit section: Taiwan")]

Main article: [Video gaming in Taiwan](/wiki/Video_gaming_in_Taiwan "Video gaming in Taiwan")

The video game industry in [Taiwan](/wiki/Taiwan "Taiwan") developed along a markedly different path from that of North America, Europe, and Japan. In the 1980s, limited copyright awareness and the high cost of legitimate software led many Taiwanese companies to focus on distributing and modifying imported titles.[[141]](#cite_note-141)

By the early 1990s, however, Taiwan began to cultivate its own game development scene, with companies like [Softstar Entertainment](/wiki/Softstar "Softstar") producing influential role-playing titles such as [The Legend of Sword and Fairy](/wiki/The_Legend_of_Sword_and_Fairy_(video_game) "The Legend of Sword and Fairy (video game)") and the [Xuan-Yuan Sword](/wiki/Xuan-Yuan_Sword "Xuan-Yuan Sword") series, both of which became cultural touchstones across the [Sinophone](/wiki/Sinophone "Sinophone") world.[[142]](#cite_note-142) The release of and transition to [Windows 95](/wiki/Windows_95 "Windows 95") initially disrupted Taiwan's PC game development, but studios adapted quickly, and by the late 1990s a wave of domestic games achieved notable commercial success.

In the 2000s, the rapid rise of broadband Internet and online gaming reshaped the market. Many studios shifted toward online games and licensed publishing, though the domestic single‑player market declined under pressure from piracy and growing development costs.[[143]](#cite_note-143) The era also marked the rise of Taiwan's first professional [esports](/wiki/Esport "Esport") events. In 2003, the [Taipei Game Show](/wiki/Taipei_Game_Show "Taipei Game Show") started attracting international developers and audiences, further embedding online games into Taiwan's youth culture.[[144]](#cite_note-144)

Today, Taiwan's game industry is characterized by a mix of smaller independent studios and established publishers, as well as its vibrant [esports](/wiki/Esports "Esports") scene, with tournaments drawing large audiences and significant sponsorships. For example, a Taiwanese gamer – named Chen Yin-hung – won the [Apex Legends championship](/wiki/Apex_Legends_Global_Series "Apex Legends Global Series") at 2025's [Esports World Cup](/wiki/Esports_World_Cup "Esports World Cup") held in [Saudi Arabia](/wiki/Saudi_Arabia "Saudi Arabia"), earning the championship and the tournament's most valuable player title, becoming the first Taiwanese player to achieve both distinctions.[[145]](#cite_note-145) In 2022, with an estimated total of 17.1 million gamers, the value of Taiwan's digital gaming market exceeded US$2.2 billion, with mobile gaming accounting for the largest share of 63.8%.[[146]](#cite_note-146) According to [Taiwan's Ministry of Culture](/wiki/Ministry_of_Culture_(Taiwan) "Ministry of Culture (Taiwan)"), the nation is both a significant consumer and a producer of online and mobile games, with a growing number of developers and art studios contributing to international projects.[[147]](#cite_note-147)

### Oceania

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_industry&action=edit&section=40 "Edit section: Oceania")]

#### Australia and New Zealand

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_industry&action=edit&section=41 "Edit section: Australia and New Zealand")]

Main articles: [Video gaming in Australia](/wiki/Video_gaming_in_Australia "Video gaming in Australia") and [Video gaming in New Zealand](/wiki/Video_gaming_in_New_Zealand "Video gaming in New Zealand")

Australia and New Zealand have an active video game industry, with several standalone developers as well as additional studios from other major developers across the globe.

## Conventions, trade shows, and conferences

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_industry&action=edit&section=42 "Edit section: Conventions, trade shows, and conferences")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/20/Gamescom_2009_-_Koelnmesse_S%C3%BCdeingang_%285473%29.jpg/250px-Gamescom_2009_-_Koelnmesse_S%C3%BCdeingang_%285473%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gamescom_2009_-_Koelnmesse_S%C3%BCdeingang_(5473).jpg)

The [Gamescom](/wiki/Gamescom "Gamescom") in [Cologne](/wiki/Cologne "Cologne"), the largest video game fair by attendance

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7a/GDC_2010_expo_floor_%284427509019%29.jpg/250px-GDC_2010_expo_floor_%284427509019%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:GDC_2010_expo_floor_(4427509019).jpg)

The expo floor at the 2010 [Game Developers Conference](/wiki/Game_Developers_Conference "Game Developers Conference")

[Gaming conventions](/wiki/Gaming_convention "Gaming convention") are an important showcase of the industry. These typically provide the means for developers and publishers to demonstrate their games directly to video game players and consumers and obtain feedback. New games are frequently introduced during these events. Some examples of each conventions include the annual [Gamescom](/wiki/Gamescom "Gamescom") in [Cologne](/wiki/Cologne "Cologne"), and numerous [PAX](/wiki/PAX_(event) "PAX (event)") events. Some publishers, developers and technology producers also have their own regular conventions, with [BlizzCon](/wiki/BlizzCon "BlizzCon"), [QuakeCon](/wiki/QuakeCon "QuakeCon"), [Nvision](/wiki/Nvision "Nvision") and the [X shows](/wiki/X_(Xbox_show) "X (Xbox show)") being prominent examples.

National trade groups that support their local video game industry often will hold [trade shows](/wiki/Trade_show "Trade show") aimed for developers and publishers to interact more directly with the video game media, and with retailers and distributors for planning future sales of products. The largest such trade show was [E3](/wiki/Electronic_Entertainment_Expo "Electronic Entertainment Expo") in Los Angeles, California is held by the [Entertainment Software Association](/wiki/Entertainment_Software_Association "Entertainment Software Association"). Other similar trade shows include [Tokyo Game Show](/wiki/Tokyo_Game_Show "Tokyo Game Show") (Japan), [Brasil Game Show](/wiki/Brasil_Game_Show "Brasil Game Show") (Brazil), [EB Games Expo](/wiki/EB_Games_Expo "EB Games Expo") (Australia), [KRI](/wiki/Russian_Game_Developers_Conference "Russian Game Developers Conference") (Russia), [ChinaJoy](/wiki/China_Digital_Entertainment_Expo_%26_Conference "China Digital Entertainment Expo & Conference") (China) and the annual [Game Developers Conference](/wiki/Game_Developers_Conference "Game Developers Conference").

The development of video games is also a topic of academic and professional interest, leading to a number of [conferences](/wiki/Academic_conference "Academic conference") for developers to share their knowledge with others. Two of the major professional conferences include the [Game Developers Conference](/wiki/Game_Developers_Conference "Game Developers Conference") (GDC), which holds multiple events through the year but with its main annual conference held in March in San Francisco, and the [D.I.C.E. Summit](/wiki/D.I.C.E._Summit "D.I.C.E. Summit") run by the [Academy of Interactive Arts & Sciences](/wiki/Academy_of_Interactive_Arts_%26_Sciences "Academy of Interactive Arts & Sciences") in February of each year in Las Vegas, Nevada.

## Media coverage and archiving

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_industry&action=edit&section=43 "Edit section: Media coverage and archiving")]

See also: [Video game journalism](/wiki/Video_game_journalism "Video game journalism") and [Video game preservation](/wiki/Video_game_preservation "Video game preservation")

The coverage of the video game industry started off with several magazines covering the topic, but as the Internet became widely available to support [new media](/wiki/New_media "New media"), much of the dedicated coverage of the video game industry has transitioned to detected websites, including *[Gamasutra](/wiki/Gamasutra "Gamasutra")*, *[IGN](/wiki/IGN "IGN")*, *[Eurogamer](/wiki/Eurogamer "Eurogamer")*, *[Polygon](/wiki/Polygon_(website) "Polygon (website)")* and *[GameSpot](/wiki/GameSpot "GameSpot")*. More recently, the effect of [social media influencers](/wiki/Social_media_influencers "Social media influencers"), video game players that create online videos or stream themselves playing games through services like [Twitch](/wiki/Twitch_(service) "Twitch (service)"), have also become a significant source for coverage of video game news from the consumer point of view.

Another facet of tracking the history of the video game industry is video game preservation, a process that is complicated due to game hardware technology that can become obsolete, dependencies on decommissioned online servers, and issues over [intellectual property](/wiki/Intellectual_property "Intellectual property") that legally restricts preservation efforts. Much of the industry's history prior to the 1983 crash has been lost, as companies affected by the crash simply threw material away, leaving little to recover today. There is better awareness of video game preservation into the 21st century, and several groups and museums have been established to collect and preserve hardware and software for the industry.[[148]](#cite_note-148)

## Recognition within the industry

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_industry&action=edit&section=44 "Edit section: Recognition within the industry")]

See also: [List of video game awards](/wiki/List_of_video_game_awards "List of video game awards")

The video game industry has a number of annual award ceremonies, commonly associated with the above conventions, trade shows, and conferences, as well as standalone award shows. Many of the dedicated video game journalism websites also have their own set of awards. Most commonly, these ceremonies are capped by the top prize, the "[Game of the Year](/wiki/List_of_Game_of_the_Year_awards "List of Game of the Year awards")".

## Trends

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_game_industry&action=edit&section=45 "Edit section: Trends")]

| --- | --- |

Players become fourth-party developers, allowing for more [open source](/wiki/Open-source_software "Open-source software") models of [game design](/wiki/Game_design "Game design"), development and engineering. Players also create [modifications](/wiki/Mod_(computer_gaming) "Mod (computer gaming)") (mods), which in some cases become just as popular as the original game for which they were created. An example of this is the game *[Counter-Strike](/wiki/Counter-Strike_(video_game) "Counter-Strike (video game)")*, which began as a mod of the video game *[Half-Life](/wiki/Half-Life_(video_game) "Half-Life (video game)")* and eventually became a very successful, published game in its own right.

While this "community of modifiers" may only add up to approximately 1% of a particular game's user base, the number of those involved will grow as more games offer modifying opportunities (such as, by releasing [source code](/wiki/Source_code "Source code")) and the video user base swells. According to Ben Sawyer, as many as 600,000 established online game community developers existed as of 2012. This effectively added a new component to the [game industry value chain](#Game_industry_value_chain) and if it continues to mature, it will integrate itself into the overall industry.[[10]](#cite_note-newmedia-10)

The industry has seen a shift towards games with [multiplayer](/wiki/Multiplayer_video_game "Multiplayer video game") facilities. A larger percentage of games on all types of platforms include some type of competitive online multiplayer capability.

In addition, the industry is experiencing further significant change driven by convergence, with technology and player comfort being the two primary reasons for this wave of industry convergence. Video games and related content can now be accessed and played on a variety of media, including: cable television, dedicated consoles, handheld devices and smartphones, through social networking sites or through an ISP, through a game developer's website, and online through a game console and/or home or office personal computer. In fact, 12% of U.S. households already make regular use of game consoles for accessing video content provided by online services such as Hulu and Netflix. In 2012, for the first time, entertainment usage passed multiplayer game usage on Xbox, meaning that users spent more time with online video and music services and applications than playing multiplayer games. This rapid type of industry convergence has caused the distinction between video game console and personal computers to disappear. A game console with high-speed microprocessors attached to a television set is, for all intents and purposes, a computer and monitor.[[149]](#cite_note-149)

As this distinction has been diminished, players' willingness to play and access content on different platforms has increased. The growing video gamer demographic accounts for this trend, as former president of the Entertainment Software Association Douglas Lowenstein explained at the 10th E3 expo, "Looking ahead, a child born in 1995, E3's inaugural year, will be 19 years old in 2014. And according to Census Bureau data, by the year 2020, there will be 174 million Americans between the ages of 5 and 44. That's 174 million Americans who will have grown up with PlayStations, Xboxes, and GameCubes from their early childhood and teenage years...What this means is that the average gamer will be both older and, given their lifetime familiarity with playing interactive games, more sophisticated and discriminating about the games they play."[[150]](#cite_note-150)

Evidence of the increasing player willingness to play video games across a variety of media and different platforms can be seen in the rise of casual gaming on smartphones, tablets, and social networking sites as 92% of all smartphone and tablet owners play games at least once a week, 45% play daily, and industry estimates predict that, by 2016, one-third of all global mobile video game revenue will come from tablets alone.[*[outdated statistic](/wiki/Wikipedia:Avoid_weasel_words "Wikipedia:Avoid weasel words")*] Apple's App Store alone has more than 90,000 game apps, a growth of 1,400% since it went online. In addition, game revenues for iOS and Android mobile devices now exceed those of both Nintendo and Sony handheld video game systems combined.[[151]](#cite_note-151)
